Q1: Honestly, my study routine was all over the place because I'm 29 and juggling so much outside of school. I tried to carve out time on weekends and late evenings after everything else was done, but it rarely worked out as planned. I'm a visual learner, so I'd print materials or sketch out diagrams when I could, but finding consistent time was always the struggle. I'd have bursts of intense studying followed by days where I couldn't touch anything.
Q2: My pattern varied wildly depending on deadlines and what was happening in my life. Some weeks I'd barely look at course materials, then I'd have marathon sessions right before something was due. I wished I could be more regular about it, but with limited resources and competing responsibilities, I had to be flexible. The anxiety of falling behind would build up, and then I'd power through everything at once.
Q3: Usually it was panic about a deadline that got me to log in. Sometimes I'd remember there was something due and rush to check what I'd missed. Occasionally I'd log in just to see if there were announcements or if I'd missed something important during the weeks I couldn't attend. It wasn't a habit so much as a response to that gnawing feeling that I was falling behind.
Q4: Not really, if I'm being honest. I only logged in maybe once a week at most, so it never became routine. The gamified tasks they introduced actually helped a bit because they made checking in feel less overwhelming and more like small wins. But between my low attendance and everything else going on, the LMS never felt like a natural part of my week.
Q5: The video lectures were incredibly helpful because I'm such a visual learner. I could watch them when I actually had time, pause them, rewind, and really absorb the material. The discussion posts were also useful because seeing how other students approached problems gave me different perspectives. Those resources let me learn on my own schedule, which was essential given my situation.
Q6: I often skipped the optional readings and some of the supplementary materials. With only about 16 hours a week to study and limited resources, I had to prioritize ruthlessly. If something didn't seem directly connected to assignments or exams, I'd push it aside. I always meant to go back to them, but there was never enough time.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see a direct connection to my grade or understanding key concepts. The gamified tasks actually helped with this because they broke things into smaller, achievable goals that gave me a sense of progress. When I could see immediate results or feel like I was building toward something, the anxiety about wasting time went away a bit.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford. Required ones created this pressure that was stressful but also motivating in a way. With optional stuff, I'd think 'maybe later' and later never came. The required activities at least gave me structure and forced me to engage even when everything felt overwhelming.
Q9: Absolutely, most of my studying happened offline. I'd download PDFs and videos when I had internet access, then study from those materials for days without logging in. I'd make handwritten notes, create visual diagrams, and work through problems on paper. My actual learning happened away from the computer much more than the LMS would ever show.
Q10: There were entire weeks where I was deeply engaged with course material but never touched the LMS. I'd have printed readings spread across my table, working through concepts, making flashcards, drawing diagrams. I'd review my notes on the bus or during lunch breaks. All that effort was invisible online, but it's how I actually learned the material well enough to score so high on exams.
Q11: All the time. I'd download videos once and watch them multiple times offline. I'd save PDFs and annotate them by hand. Given my limited and unreliable internet situation, downloading everything in one session and then studying offline for days was just practical. One login might represent a week's worth of actual studying.
Q12: There were definitely harder periods, especially when anxiety spiked or when I had competing responsibilities piling up. Missing so many classes made it harder because I'd feel disconnected and behind. Sometimes the sheer volume of what I'd missed felt paralyzing. My resilience usually kicked in eventually, but getting started again after a gap was always the hardest part.
Q13: Fatigue and competing responsibilities were constant factors. Being 29 with limited resources means school isn't my only priority, even though I desperately want it to be. When I was exhausted, I'd log in and just stare at the screen without really absorbing anything. The workload felt impossible sometimes, and I'd have to make hard choices about what to engage with.
Q14: Yes, more often than I'd like to admit. Sometimes I'd open videos or readings but my mind would be elsewhere, worrying about other things. I'd scroll through materials without really processing them. The anxiety would be so high that I couldn't focus, even though I was technically 'engaging' with the content. Those sessions felt wasted.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even knowing they were important. The anxiety about doing them imperfectly would paralyze me. I'd tell myself I needed more time to prepare, but really I was just avoiding the stress of starting. It's why my assignment completion wasn't great even though I understood the material well enough to ace exams.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my only structure. They created these intense bursts of activity where I'd finally do everything I'd been putting off. Without a deadline looming, I'd drift. The pressure was stressful but also necessary because otherwise I might never have engaged at all. My LMS activity probably spiked dramatically right before due dates.
Q17: Feedback definitely affected me, sometimes too much. Good feedback gave me confidence to keep going, but critical feedback would send my anxiety through the roof. I'd sometimes avoid checking feedback for days because I was scared of what it might say. When it was constructive, it helped me focus my limited study time on what actually mattered.
Q18: The course structure was challenging for someone with my attendance issues. Missing 80% of classes meant I was constantly playing catch-up. The gamified elements helped because they gave me smaller goals to work toward. But overall, the structure assumed more regular participation than I could manage, which made everything feel harder.
Q19: The availability of video lectures made engagement much easier because I could learn visually on my own time. What made it harder was when activities required being present for live sessions or building on previous weeks' attendance. The gamified tasks actually made things easier because they felt less overwhelming than traditional assignments.
Q20: I think those students might be like me in some ways, exploring and trying to understand but struggling to translate that into completed work. High anxiety can make you obsessively check things without actually producing anything. Or they might be dealing with perfectionism, where they engage with everything but can't bring themselves to submit imperfect work.
Q21: That's basically my story. My LMS activity was minimal, but I scored nearly perfect on exams. I was learning constantly, just not in ways the system could track. Downloading materials once and studying them extensively offline, using handwritten notes, processing information visually on paper. The LMS only saw the tip of the iceberg.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns of when people log in and what they click on, but they miss the depth of engagement entirely. They can't see me studying from printed notes at 2 AM or rewatching a downloaded video for the third time. They capture presence but not understanding, clicks but not comprehension. The emotional and cognitive work is invisible.
Q23: Being engaged means genuinely wrestling with the material, not just going through the motions. It's when I'm drawing diagrams to understand concepts, when I'm thinking about course content while doing other things, when I care about actually learning rather than just passing. It's mental and emotional investment, not just logging in and clicking around.
Q24: The LMS would never show how much I studied offline, how many times I reviewed materials after downloading them once, or the anxiety that both drove and hindered my engagement. It wouldn't capture my visual learning strategies, my handwritten notes, or the genuine happiness I felt when concepts finally clicked. My high exam scores came from real engagement that happened entirely outside the system.
